title: Concrete foundation cost: monolithic, stem wall, basement
excerpt: A concrete foundation runs $5-$37 per square foot in 2026 — monolithic slab cheapest at $5-$14, basement most expensive at $20-$37 installed.
geoAnchor: Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed foundation, slab, driveway, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records and contractor invoice samples — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, a poured concrete foundation lands between $5 and $37 per square foot installed depending on type: monolithic slab at $5-$14, stem wall (slab plus short footing wall) at $9-$22, full crawl space at $11-$25, and full basement at $20-$37. On a 2,000-square-foot footprint that range stretches from a $10,000 monolithic slab to a $74,000 basement. The number is driven less by the concrete itself ($150-$180 per cubic yard delivered) and more by excavation, soil conditions, waterproofing, drainage, and code-required reinforcement. Foundation pricing varies more between regions than any other residential concrete category — frost-line depth and soil class explain most of the spread.

faqs.question: How much does a concrete foundation cost for a 2,000-square-foot house in 2026?
faqs.answer: A 2,000-square-foot footprint runs $10,000-$74,000 for the foundation alone in 2026. Monolithic slab lands at $10,000-$28,000, stem wall at $18,000-$44,000, crawl space at $22,000-$50,000, and full basement at $40,000-$74,000. The single biggest driver is foundation type — basements cost 3-7x what a monolithic slab costs because of excavation depth, wall pour, waterproofing, and drainage requirements.

faqs.question: What is the cheapest type of concrete foundation?
faqs.answer: A monolithic slab — also called a slab-on-grade or thickened-edge slab — is the cheapest concrete foundation at $5-$14 per square foot installed. The slab and footing are poured in one continuous lift, eliminating a separate footing dig and a second concrete delivery. Monolithic slabs are most common in warm climates (Florida, Texas, Arizona) where frost-line depth doesn't require a footing below grade.

faqs.question: Is a basement worth the extra cost vs a crawl space?
faqs.answer: On a cost-per-finished-square-foot basis, a basement is usually the cheapest way to add living space to a house. A full basement adds roughly $20-$30 per square foot to foundation cost vs a crawl space. Finishing that basement later runs another $30-$80 per square foot. Compared to building the same square footage above grade ($150-$300+ per square foot), a finished basement delivers usable space at 30-50 percent the cost of an addition.

faqs.question: Why do foundation costs vary so much by region?
faqs.answer: Frost-line depth is the biggest single driver. The International Code Council requires footings below the local frost line. In southern Florida that's 0-12 inches; in northern Minnesota it's 60-72 inches. Every additional foot of footing depth adds excavation, formwork, concrete volume, and labor. Soil class matters next — expansive clay, high water tables, and bedrock all add $3-$15 per square foot to base pricing.

faqs.question: How much of a foundation cost is concrete vs labor?
faqs.answer: Concrete material is typically 18-28 percent of total foundation cost. Ready-mix concrete delivered runs $150-$180 per cubic yard in 2026. The remaining 72-82 percent breaks down across excavation, formwork, rebar, labor, waterproofing, drain tile, backfill, and inspection. On a $25,000 stem wall foundation, the concrete itself is roughly $5,500; the rest is everything that touches the concrete before, during, and after it cures.

faqs.question: Does a thicker foundation slab cost much more?
faqs.answer: Going from a 4-inch to a 6-inch monolithic slab adds roughly $0.80-$1.40 per square foot in concrete cost (50 percent more cubic yardage) plus modest extra labor. On a 1,800-square-foot footprint, that's an $1,800-$3,200 upgrade. For loads above standard residential (heavy garage equipment, slab-on-grade home with no perimeter beam, expansive soils), 6 inches is often code-required or specified by an engineer.

faqs.question: What does foundation waterproofing add to the cost?
faqs.answer: Foundation waterproofing on a basement or deep stem wall runs $3-$11 per square foot of wall surface in 2026. Sprayed liquid asphalt is cheapest at $3-$5 per square foot; spray-applied elastomeric membrane runs $5-$8; full self-adhered bentonite or dimple-board systems run $7-$11. According to the Federal Highway Administration, the long-term cost of remediating a wet basement runs 3-8x the cost of installing proper drainage and waterproofing at construction.

faqs.question: How long does a concrete foundation last?
faqs.answer: A properly poured and reinforced concrete foundation lasts 80-100+ years before requiring structural replacement. The Portland Cement Association cites 100 years as the design life of residential foundations built to current ACI standards. What does need replacement on a shorter schedule is waterproofing (25-30 years) and perimeter drainage (40-50 years). Failure modes that shorten foundation life are settlement (poor compaction), heave (expansive soils), and freeze-thaw spalling at exposed walls.

howToSteps.name: Site prep and excavation
howToSteps.text: Topsoil is stripped, the building pad is graded, and excavation goes to depth. Monolithic slab requires only a 4-6 inch dig; a full basement requires 8-10 feet of excavation plus a working perimeter.

howToSteps.name: Subgrade compaction and base
howToSteps.text: The exposed subgrade is compacted in lifts to a target density (typically 95 percent standard Proctor). A 4-6 inch compacted gravel base is added under the slab; a capillary break and vapor barrier go on top per the local code.

howToSteps.name: Footing pour
howToSteps.text: On stem wall, crawl space, and basement foundations, the perimeter footing is poured first. Footings are sized to spread the load below the frost line; rebar is placed in 2 horizontal runs minimum per ACI 318.

howToSteps.name: Wall formwork and pour
howToSteps.text: For stem walls and basements, wall forms are set on top of the cured footing. Rebar is tied vertically and horizontally per engineering spec, then concrete is pumped in lifts and vibrated to consolidate.

howToSteps.name: Slab pour
howToSteps.text: On monolithic, the slab and thickened footing edge are poured in one continuous lift over the vapor barrier and mesh/rebar. On stem wall and basement, the slab is poured between the cured stem walls after backfill if used.

howToSteps.name: Waterproofing and drainage
howToSteps.text: Below-grade walls get waterproofing membrane applied to the exterior face. Perimeter drain tile is set in a gravel envelope at the footing level and routed to daylight or a sump.

howToSteps.name: Backfill and final grade
howToSteps.text: Approved backfill material is placed in lifts and compacted, with the final grade sloping a minimum 6 inches of drop in the first 10 feet away from the foundation per IRC R401.3.

content:
Quick Answer: A residential concrete foundation costs $5-$37 per square foot of footprint installed in 2026. Monolithic slab is cheapest at $5-$14, stem wall at $9-$22, crawl space at $11-$25, and full basement at $20-$37. On a 2,000-square-foot house that range is $10,000-$74,000. Frost-line depth, soil class, and waterproofing drive most of the variation.

Foundation is the single largest concrete line item in a new home build and the hardest to compare quote-to-quote because there are four meaningfully different types, each priced on a different scope of work. Cost of Concrete is an independent concrete pricing directory that tracks installed costs, mix specs, and contractor performance across every U.S. state. Pricing is sourced from real homeowner-reported job records and contractor invoice samples, not contractor self-promotion. This guide breaks down 2026 foundation pricing by type, what's actually in the number, and where the regional spread comes from.

Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed foundation, slab, driveway, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records and contractor invoice samples — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, a poured concrete foundation lands between $5 and $37 per square foot installed depending on type: monolithic slab at $5-$14, stem wall (slab plus short footing wall) at $9-$22, full crawl space at $11-$25, and full basement at $20-$37. On a 2,000-square-foot footprint that range stretches from a $10,000 monolithic slab to a $74,000 basement. The number is driven less by the concrete itself ($150-$180 per cubic yard delivered) and more by excavation, soil conditions, waterproofing, drainage, and code-required reinforcement. Foundation pricing varies more between regions than any other residential concrete category — frost-line depth and soil class explain most of the spread.

2026 pricing by foundation type

Pricing below is national typical installed for a single-family residential foundation, complete through backfill. Excludes interior slab finishing, basement waterproofing system selection, and any structural engineering required for difficult soils.

Foundation type$ per sq ft of footprint1,500 sq ft footprint2,000 sq ft footprint2,500 sq ft footprint
Monolithic slab$5-$14$7,500-$21,000$10,000-$28,000$12,500-$35,000
Stem wall (24-36 in)$9-$22$13,500-$33,000$18,000-$44,000$22,500-$55,000
Crawl space (4 ft)$11-$25$16,500-$37,500$22,000-$50,000$27,500-$62,500
Full basement (8 ft)$20-$37$30,000-$55,500$40,000-$74,000$50,000-$92,500

The published per-square-foot rate is always quoted on the building footprint, not on wall surface area. A 2,000-square-foot footprint with a full 8-foot basement actually involves roughly 1,400-1,600 square feet of wall surface plus the 2,000-square-foot slab — but the industry convention prices it back to the footprint number for apples-to-apples comparison.

Monolithic slab: $5-$14 per square foot

A monolithic slab pours the floor slab and the perimeter thickened footing in one continuous lift. Most common in warm climates where the frost line is shallow or non-existent. Typical spec: 4-inch interior slab, 12-24 inch deep thickened edge, wire mesh or fiber reinforcement, 3,000-3,500 PSI ready-mix.

What drives a monolithic slab to the high end of the range:

  • Soil class: expansive clay or organic soils may require a post-tension cable system or thicker engineered slab
  • Termite protection: required in Southern climate zones, $0.30-$0.80 per square foot
  • Code-required perimeter beam depth: some jurisdictions require 36-inch perimeter beams even on monolithic slabs
  • Post-tension cables: add $1-$3 per square foot, common in expansive soil areas

For full slab pricing isolated from foundation context, see Cost of Concrete's concrete slab cost guide.

Stem wall foundation: $9-$22 per square foot

A stem wall consists of a continuous footing below frost line plus a short perimeter wall (typically 24-36 inches above grade or above footing) supporting the floor system. The interior is either an interior slab on grade or a wood-framed floor over a crawl space depending on the design.

Cost components for a 2,000-square-foot stem wall foundation typically break down:

  • Excavation and grading: $1.50-$3.50 per sq ft of footprint
  • Footing (concrete + rebar + forms): $2.00-$4.50
  • Stem wall (concrete + forms + rebar): $3.50-$8.00
  • Interior slab on grade (if included): $2.00-$5.00
  • Waterproofing (below-grade portion): $0.30-$1.50
  • Backfill, compaction, finish grade: $1.00-$2.50

Stem walls are the most common new-construction foundation in mixed climates. The footing-below-frost-line requirement set by the International Code Council in IRC R403.1.4 makes them the default solution between roughly Latitude 30 and Latitude 42 in the U.S.

Crawl space foundation: $11-$25 per square foot

A crawl space is a deeper stem wall — typically 36-48 inches between finished interior grade and the underside of the floor framing — designed to allow access for plumbing, HVAC ducts, and electrical without a poured floor slab. The interior is graded soil or a vapor-barrier-covered surface, not a structural slab.

Crawl space pricing adds roughly $2-$4 per square foot over stem wall pricing for the additional excavation, wall height, and code-required ventilation or conditioning system. The 2024 IRC update increasingly favors closed (conditioned) crawl spaces over vented designs, which adds insulation, vapor barrier upgrades, and a sealed access door — another $1.50-$3 per square foot.

Full basement: $20-$37 per square foot

A full basement is the most expensive foundation type and also the most expensive concrete project a typical homeowner ever commissions. Eight to nine feet of finished interior height requires roughly 8.5-10 feet of excavation depth, perimeter walls 8-10 inches thick reinforced with vertical rebar, a 4-6 inch slab pour over compacted base and vapor barrier, and a complete exterior waterproofing system.

Typical 2,000-square-foot full basement cost breakdown:

Line itemTypical cost% of total
Excavation + haul-off$8,000-$14,00015-20%
Footings (concrete + rebar + forms)$5,000-$9,00010-12%
Basement walls (poured concrete or block)$15,000-$25,00030-35%
Basement slab$5,000-$9,00010-12%
Waterproofing membrane + drain tile$5,000-$10,00010-15%
Backfill, compaction, sump pit, egress$2,000-$7,0005-10%
Total$40,000-$74,000100%

Poured concrete walls vs concrete masonry unit (CMU) block walls: poured walls run roughly 10-25 percent more than CMU block but are significantly more water-resistant and faster to construct. According to the Portland Cement Association, poured concrete is the dominant new-construction basement wall in residential builds north of Latitude 40.

What drives regional price variation

Foundation pricing varies more by region than any other residential concrete category. The four big drivers:

Frost-line depth

Local code requires footings below the published frost line. In Miami the frost line is 0; in International Falls, Minnesota, it's 72 inches. Every additional foot of footing depth adds roughly $1.50-$3.50 per linear foot of footing in excavation, formwork, and concrete. On a 200-foot perimeter foundation, the difference between a 12-inch and a 60-inch footing is $4,800-$11,200.

Soil class

Expansive clay, organic soils, high water tables, and bedrock all add cost. A geotechnical report (typically $1,500-$4,500) is increasingly required before pricing. Expansive clay alone can add $3-$8 per square foot of footprint via post-tension cables, drilled piers, or engineered slab specs.

Local labor and mix-design pricing

Ready-mix concrete varies from $130-$220 per cubic yard nationally; foundation crew labor ranges from $45-$120 per hour. The Northeast, San Francisco Bay, Seattle, and Honolulu are the most expensive metros; the South-Central U.S. (Texas, Oklahoma, Tennessee, Mississippi) is among the cheapest.

Waterproofing spec

Waterproofing pricing varies 3-4x depending on system. Sprayed liquid asphalt is cheap ($3-$5 per sq ft of wall surface) but short-lived; sheet-applied or bentonite systems run $7-$11. Drain tile pipe in a gravel envelope is required by code on most basements and is $25-$50 per linear foot installed.

How a concrete foundation is installed

Same baseline sequence for all foundation types; basement and crawl space add wall pour and waterproofing steps between footing and slab.

  1. Site prep and excavation. Topsoil is stripped, the building pad is graded, and excavation goes to depth. Monolithic slab requires only a 4-6 inch dig; a full basement requires 8-10 feet of excavation plus a working perimeter.
  2. Subgrade compaction and base. The exposed subgrade is compacted in lifts to a target density (typically 95 percent standard Proctor). A 4-6 inch compacted gravel base is added under the slab; a capillary break and vapor barrier go on top per the local code.
  3. Footing pour. On stem wall, crawl space, and basement foundations, the perimeter footing is poured first. Footings are sized to spread the load below the frost line; rebar is placed in 2 horizontal runs minimum per ACI 318.
  4. Wall formwork and pour. For stem walls and basements, wall forms are set on top of the cured footing. Rebar is tied vertically and horizontally per engineering spec, then concrete is pumped in lifts and vibrated to consolidate.
  5. Slab pour. On monolithic, the slab and thickened footing edge are poured in one continuous lift over the vapor barrier and mesh/rebar. On stem wall and basement, the slab is poured between the cured stem walls after backfill if used.
  6. Waterproofing and drainage. Below-grade walls get waterproofing membrane applied to the exterior face. Perimeter drain tile is set in a gravel envelope at the footing level and routed to daylight or a sump.
  7. Backfill and final grade. Approved backfill material is placed in lifts and compacted, with the final grade sloping a minimum 6 inches of drop in the first 10 feet away from the foundation per IRC R401.3.

Total project timeline for a 2,000-square-foot foundation: monolithic slab 1-2 weeks, stem wall 2-3 weeks, crawl space 2-3 weeks, full basement 3-5 weeks weather permitting.

How to evaluate a foundation quote

A foundation quote should always specify the foundation type, slab thickness, footing dimensions, wall thickness, rebar schedule, PSI of concrete, waterproofing system, drain tile presence, and backfill spec. If a quote is just a lump sum with no spec sheet, ask for the breakdown. The American Concrete Institute publishes ACI 332 (residential code requirements) which any quote should claim conformance to.

For full guidance on reading concrete quotes line-by-line, see Cost of Concrete's contractor quote guide. To compare local foundation pricing against verified contractors in your city, browse the national best concrete contractors directory or your concrete foundations service hub.

Frequently asked questions

How much does a concrete foundation cost for a 2,000-square-foot house in 2026?

A 2,000-square-foot footprint runs $10,000-$74,000 for the foundation alone in 2026. Monolithic slab lands at $10,000-$28,000, stem wall at $18,000-$44,000, crawl space at $22,000-$50,000, and full basement at $40,000-$74,000. The single biggest driver is foundation type — basements cost 3-7x what a monolithic slab costs because of excavation depth, wall pour, waterproofing, and drainage requirements.

What is the cheapest type of concrete foundation?

A monolithic slab — also called a slab-on-grade or thickened-edge slab — is the cheapest concrete foundation at $5-$14 per square foot installed. The slab and footing are poured in one continuous lift, eliminating a separate footing dig and a second concrete delivery. Monolithic slabs are most common in warm climates (Florida, Texas, Arizona) where frost-line depth doesn't require a footing below grade.

On a cost-per-finished-square-foot basis, a basement is usually the cheapest way to add living space to a house. A full basement adds roughly $20-$30 per square foot to foundation cost vs a crawl space. Finishing that basement later runs another $30-$80 per square foot. Compared to building the same square footage above grade ($150-$300+ per square foot), a finished basement delivers usable space at 30-50 percent the cost of an addition.

Frost-line depth is the biggest single driver. The International Code Council requires footings below the local frost line. In southern Florida that's 0-12 inches; in northern Minnesota it's 60-72 inches. Every additional foot of footing depth adds excavation, formwork, concrete volume, and labor. Soil class matters next — expansive clay, high water tables, and bedrock all add $3-$15 per square foot to base pricing.

Concrete material is typically 18-28 percent of total foundation cost. Ready-mix concrete delivered runs $150-$180 per cubic yard in 2026. The remaining 72-82 percent breaks down across excavation, formwork, rebar, labor, waterproofing, drain tile, backfill, and inspection. On a $25,000 stem wall foundation, the concrete itself is roughly $5,500; the rest is everything that touches the concrete before, during, and after it cures.

Going from a 4-inch to a 6-inch monolithic slab adds roughly $0.80-$1.40 per square foot in concrete cost (50 percent more cubic yardage) plus modest extra labor. Foundation waterproofing on a basement or deep stem wall runs $3-$11 per square foot of wall surface in 2026. Sprayed liquid asphalt is cheapest at $3-$5 per square foot; spray-applied elastomeric membrane runs $5-$8; full self-adhered bentonite or dimple-board systems run $7-$11. According to the Federal Highway Administration, the long-term cost of remediating a wet basement runs 3-8x the cost of installing proper drainage and waterproofing at construction.

How long does a concrete foundation last?

A properly poured and reinforced concrete foundation lasts 80-100+ years before requiring structural replacement. Failure modes that shorten foundation life are settlement (poor compaction), heave (expansive soils), and freeze-thaw spalling at exposed walls.

Key takeaways

  • 2026 national foundation pricing: $5-$37 per square foot of footprint depending on type — monolithic cheapest, basement most expensive.
  • On a 2,000-square-foot house, that's $10,000 (monolithic slab) up to $74,000 (full basement).
  • Concrete material is only 18-28 percent of foundation cost; excavation, formwork, rebar, waterproofing, and drainage make up the rest.
  • Frost-line depth and soil class drive most regional variation; the South-Central U.S. is cheapest, Northeast and West Coast are most expensive.
  • A full basement is the cheapest way to add usable square footage to a house at the build stage — 30-50 percent the cost of an equivalent above-grade addition.
  • Waterproofing is the single highest-leverage line item — $3-$11 per square foot at install vs 3-8x more for remediation later.

Need a contractor whose foundation quote matches these numbers? Cost of Concrete lists vetted contractors by city, sourced from real homeowner-reported pricing. Browse the national directory at best concrete contractors or open your city's page to compare local foundation pricing line by line against the national ranges above.
